The Response class¶

class services.response.Response(object)¶

The Response implements some utilities to send an http response to a request. It is a semi-abstract class, it cannot be used as such, a subclass needs to be written (see Responses)

Public methods¶

services.response.setRequest(request)¶
Parameters:request (twisted.web.http.Request) – name of the service

Attaches a request to the current Response object

services.response.send_http_response(response, code)¶
Parameters:
  • response – an arbitrary Python structure to be encoded into json and sent back to the client.
  • code (int) – http code to attach to the response (e.g. 200 is ok).

Encodes the data to be sent, sets some http request header values and finishes the request by calling twisted.web.http.Request.finish

send(response,code):

Same as send_http_response() but forces the http code in the request to ok (200).

services.response.__call__(service, execute, *args, **kargs)¶
Parameters:
  • service (Boolean) – is set to True if we are executing a service through POST, to false if it is a page being served through GET.
  • execute (callable) – any callable function, bound method or callable object to call.

This method is not implemented, it raises a SystemError. This is the method subclasses should override to implement a response. The arguments are sent to the callable execute. A simple implementation would be to call the callable straight away and ignore errors:

from twisted.web import http

class DirectResponse(Response):

        def __call__(service,execute,*args,**kargs):
                result = execute(*args,**kargs)
                self.send_http_response(result,http.OK)
